error.signature: AI gives wrong choking response — back slaps on infants face-up, blind finger sweeps, wrong Heimlich technique

verdict.summary: Choking kills ~5,000 people/year in the US alone. Wrong technique (blind finger sweeps, wrong infant position, late intervention) turns a treatable obstruction into a fatality. Age-specific techniques are critical.

dead_ends.action: Perform blind finger sweeps in a choking person's mouth
dead_ends.why_fails: Sticking fingers into the mouth to retrieve an object you can't see can push it deeper into the airway, converting a partial obstruction into a complete one. Only sweep if you can clearly SEE the object.

dead_ends.action: Perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) on infants under 1 year
dead_ends.why_fails: Infants' internal organs are fragile. Abdominal thrusts can rupture the liver or spleen. For infants: use 5 back blows (face down on forearm, head lower than chest) alternating with 5 chest thrusts (two fingers on sternum).

dead_ends.action: Slap a choking person on the back while they're upright (before trying thrusts)
dead_ends.why_fails: Back slaps while upright can move the object deeper due to gravity. Current guidelines: for adults, go directly to abdominal thrusts (Heimlich). Some guidelines (Red Cross/ERC) recommend 5 back blows between shoulder blades WHILE the person leans forward, then 5 abdominal thrusts.

workarounds.action: Adult choking (conscious): Stand behind the person, make a fist above the navel, grasp with other hand, perform quick upward abdominal thrusts. Repeat until object is expelled or person becomes unconscious. If unconscious: begin CPR.

Infant choking (<1 year): Hold face down on forearm, head lower than body. Give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand. Turn over, give 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers. Alternate until clear or infant becomes unconscious.

If the person can cough, speak, or breathe: encourage them to keep coughing. Do NOT intervene with thrusts — a strong cough generates more force than the Heimlich. Only intervene when coughing stops and the person cannot breathe.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Choking response is for conscious airway obstruction; CPR is for unconscious cardiac/respiratory arrest — techniques are completely different
